label: Emergence
definition: The appearance of properties or behaviors at the level of a whole system that its parts do not have in isolation; central to debates about consciousness, complex systems, and the surprising capabilities of large AI models.

body.text: Emergence is the idea that a system can have properties, patterns, or behaviors that its components, taken individually, do not possess, and that arise from the way those components are organized and interact. Wetness is not a property of a single water molecule but of many together; the coordinated swirl of a flock is not a property of any one bird. The concept has a long history, with the term coined by George Henry Lewes in the nineteenth century and developed by the British Emergentists of the early twentieth, such as Samuel Alexander and C. D. Broad. The guiding intuition is that "more is different", the title of a 1972 essay by the physicist Philip Anderson, meaning that new levels of organization can obey their own principles not readily deducible from the laws governing the parts.

Philosophers standardly distinguish two grades of the concept, a distinction sharpened by Mark Bedau and others. Weak emergence describes properties that do arise entirely from the interactions of the parts and are in principle derivable from them, but only by simulating or running the system, not by any shortcut; the global pattern is unexpected and novel relative to the parts yet remains fully grounded in them. Most emergence in physics, biology, and computer science is weak in this sense: traffic jams, ant-colony foraging, the behavior of a cellular automaton like Conway's Game of Life. Strong emergence is the stronger and far more contested claim that the higher-level property is genuinely novel in a way that is not even in principle reducible to or deducible from the lower level, and that it may exert downward causation on the parts. Whether strong emergence is real, or even coherent, is disputed; many scientists accept weak emergence freely while regarding strong emergence with suspicion.

In the philosophy of mind, emergence is one of the main options for situating consciousness in nature. An emergentist holds that subjective experience arises from sufficiently complex and organized physical processes in the brain. If consciousness is weakly emergent, it is fully fixed by the physical facts and the explanatory work is to show how, which is broadly the physicalist hope. If it is strongly emergent, then experience is a genuinely new feature of reality that the physical facts do not entail, which is close to the property dualism some defenders of the hard-problem-of-consciousness endorse, and it is one way of articulating why qualia might resist reduction. Emergence is attractive here because it promises novelty without invoking anything supernatural, but critics charge that calling consciousness emergent often relabels the mystery rather than explaining it, and that strong emergence in particular sits uneasily with the causal closure of the physical world.

Emergence matters for AI both as an engineering observation and as a conceptual frame. Empirically, large models exhibit capabilities that were not explicitly trained for and that appear, sometimes abruptly, as scale increases, behavior often described as emergent, though researchers debate how much of the apparent sharpness is an artifact of how the metrics are measured rather than a real phase change. The deeper question is whether genuinely new kinds of competence, or even mind, could emerge from scaling and organizing computation, which connects emergence to the prospects for artificial-general-intelligence and to whether the computational-theory-of-mind can explain everything mental as the weak emergence of high-level pattern from low-level processing. Emergence is thus invoked on both sides: as the optimistic expectation that organizing enough computation will yield qualitatively new intelligence, and as the cautious reminder, from the strong-emergence and hard-problem literature, that some properties of mind may not follow from the parts however cleverly they are arranged.
